region_of_origin: China; widely cultivated and consumed across Southeast Asia including Singapore

ontology.foodex2_description: Bok choy (Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis), raw

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw bok choy is approximately 70 g (about 1 cup chopped), commonly used as a salad base or added to soups and stir-fries before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: White Chinese cabbage (Bok choy), raw — Singapore Health Promotion Board Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for White Chinese cabbage (Bok choy), raw (cr_id F14010032). USDA FoodData Central entry 169975 (Cabbage, chinese [pak-choi], raw) used to cross-validate and supplement micronutrient fields not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous cruciferous leafy vegetable reference: cabbage, raw, GI ~23
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw bok choy found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 23 is estimated from the closest analogous cruciferous leafy vegetable (raw cabbage, GI ~23) per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 70 g serving = (23 × 2.18 × 0.70) / 100 ≈ 0.4, which is negligible. This is consistent with bok choy's very low carbohydrate content (2.18 g/100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (65 mg/100 g) and high potassium (252 mg/100 g) providing a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Raw bok choy is highly suitable for hypertensive patients due to its low sodium content (65 mg/100 g) and high potassium (252 mg/100 g); patients should avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or oyster sauce during preparation, which would negate the inherent sodium advantage.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.18 g/100 g), low GI (~23), and negligible glycaemic load per serving (~0.4)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw bok choy is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given its very low carbohydrate content (2.18 g/100 g), low estimated GI (~23), and negligible glycaemic load per serving; it can be consumed freely as part of a low-glycaemic dietary pattern, provided high-sugar sauces are avoided during cooking.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and presence of folate (66 mcg DFE/100 g) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw bok choy is cardioprotective due to its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium (252 mg/100 g), and folate content (66 mcg DFE/100 g), which supports homocysteine metabolism; it is suitable for daily inclusion in a heart-healthy diet without restriction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (~45 mg/100 g) and calcium (105 mg/100 g) with sulforaphane precursors providing antioxidant and anti-inflammatory benefits
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bok choy is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its vitamin C content (~45 mg/100 g) supporting collagen synthesis and immune function, and its calcium (105 mg/100 g) protecting bone density in patients on corticosteroids; light steaming or blanching is preferred over high-heat cooking to preserve vitamin C.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White Chinese cabbage, commonly known as bok choy or pak choi (小白菜; Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis), is a leafy cruciferous vegetable widely consumed across China, Singapore, and Southeast Asia. In its raw form, it features dark green outer leaves with crisp, succulent white stems and a mild, slightly sweet flavour with a subtle peppery note characteristic of the Brassica family. Raw bok choy is extremely low in calories (13 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrates (2.18 g/100 g), making it one of the most nutrient-dense, low-energy vegetables available. It provides meaningful amounts of calcium (105 mg/100 g), potassium (252 mg/100 g), vitamin C (~45 mg/100 g), vitamin A (~243 mcg RAE/100 g), and folate (~66 mcg DFE/100 g). Its sodium content is low at 65 mg/100 g, and it contains negligible fat (0.2 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol. Clinically, bok choy is suitable for individuals managing hypertension (favourable Na:K ratio), Type 2 Diabetes (very low GI ~23, negligible glycaemic load), and cardiovascular disease (negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, cardioprotective micronutrients). It is a staple in Singaporean and Chinese home cooking, typically stir-fried with garlic, blanched and served with oyster sauce, or added to soups and hotpots. Raw consumption is less common but occurs in salads and as a fresh garnish. All dietary flags apply: vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is bok choy good for diabetics?
How many calories in raw bok choy?
Is pak choi low in sodium?
What is the GI of bok choy?
Is bok choy suitable for hypertension?
What nutrients does bok choy contain?
Can I eat bok choy raw?
Is bok choy high in calcium?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F14010032, confidence 0.95), an approved primary regional source. Micronutrient values cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central entry 169975 (Cabbage, chinese [pak-choi], raw). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2021, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual fields for a Singaporean/Chinese food are present.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.03 g), monounsaturated fat (0.02 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.09 g), trans fat (0.0 g), and detailed micronutrients (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central entry 169975 (Cabbage, chinese [pak-choi], raw) as HPB does not report these fields; values are consistent with the HPB primary data. 2. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg and vitamin B12 is 0.0 mg: confirmed zero per HPB and USDA FoodData Central — bok choy is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol or vitamin B12. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg: confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for raw bok choy. Trans fat is 0.0 g: naturally zero for a raw leafy vegetable. 3. GI of 23 is an analogous estimate based on raw cabbage (closest cruciferous leafy vegetable) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for raw bok choy was found in the approved GI database. 4. The input record listed 'keto' as a dietary tag; this is supported by the very low net carbohydrate content (2.18 g/100 g minus 1.0 g fibre = ~1.18 g net carbs/100 g). 5. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible best-match entries; FoodEx2 code A00LC is the best available match for bok choy in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Reviewers should verify FoodEx2 code against the latest EFSA FoodEx2 browser if precision is required.
